rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns Organisations to Prepare for Incoming Vulnerability Patch Wave
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns Organisations to Prepare for Incoming Vulnerability Patch Wave
rows.summary: On 1 May 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published guidance urging organisations to ready themselves for a significant wave of security patches. The NCSC said the patches will address vulnerabilities rooted in decades of accumulated technical debt. The centre framed the update as a warning about the scale and speed of remediation work organisations should expect. The NCSC published the guidance as a blog post on its official website.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation Framework for AI Misuse in Fraud and Cybercrime
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation Framework for AI Misuse in Fraud and Cybercrime
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published an evaluation framework addressing AI misuse in fraud and cybercrime. The framework sets out a structured approach for assessing how AI systems can be exploited to facilitate fraudulent and cybercriminal activity. AISI released the framework as a blog post on its official website. The publication reflects the institute's ongoing work to identify and measure risks posed by AI capabilities in the context of criminal misuse. No financial penalties or regulatory actions were announced alongside the release.

rows.headline: UK AISI Funds 60 Projects to Advance AI Alignment Research
rows.title: UK AISI Funds 60 Projects to Advance AI Alignment Research
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute announced funding for 60 projects aimed at advancing AI alignment research. The initiative targets research into ensuring AI systems behave in accordance with intended goals and human values. AISI published details of the funded projects on its official blog, outlining the scope of work supported under the programme. The announcement reflects the institute's continued focus on AI safety research as a core part of its mandate.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published its evaluation of the cyber capabilities of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 model. The assessment formed part of AISI's ongoing programme of frontier AI safety testing. AISI conducts pre-deployment evaluations in coordination with AI developers to assess potential risks across defined capability domains. The publication covers findings specific to GPT-5.5's performance in cyber-relevant tasks. No further details from the source were available at time of processing.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Benchmark for Measuring AI Agent Container Breakout Capabilities
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Benchmark for Measuring AI Agent Container Breakout Capabilities
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post introducing a benchmark designed to measure whether AI agents can escape their sandboxes. The benchmark focuses on container breakout capabilities and aims to provide a safe method for evaluating this risk. AISI developed the tool to assess how effectively AI agents can breach the isolated environments used to contain them. The publication addresses growing concerns about AI systems operating beyond their intended boundaries.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Blog on the First AI Safety Summit
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Blog on the First AI Safety Summit
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post reviewing the first AI Safety Summit. The post appears on the AISI website and addresses the inaugural summit held in 2023. The blog is attributed to AISI and is categorised as an official publication on the institute's domain. No further details about the blog's specific findings, conclusions, or named authors are available in the source record provided.

rows.headline: UK AISI Launches Alignment Project with GBP 15 Million Fund for AI Alignment Research
rows.title: UK AISI Launches Alignment Project with GBP 15 Million Fund for AI Alignment Research
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute announced the Alignment Project, a global fund of over GBP 15 million dedicated to AI alignment research. The fund targets research into ensuring AI systems behave in accordance with human intentions and values. AISI described the initiative as a global effort, indicating participation or funding opportunities beyond the United Kingdom. The announcement was published as a blog post on the AISI website. No further details on grant structures, recipient organisations, or application processes were included in the source material.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published its evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview's cyber capabilities. The assessment examined the model's performance across cyber-relevant tasks and was conducted by AISI, the UK government body responsible for evaluating frontier AI systems for safety risks. The evaluation focused specifically on the cyber domain, assessing what the model can and cannot do in that context. AISI published the findings on its official blog, continuing its programme of pre-deployment and post-deployment model evaluations.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation of AI Models' Willingness to Sabotage Safety Research
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation of AI Models' Willingness to Sabotage Safety Research
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post examining whether AI models would sabotage AI safety research. The evaluation assessed model behaviour in scenarios where undermining safety research was a possible action. AISI conducted the work as part of its broader programme to identify risks posed by advanced AI systems. The post presents findings on how current models responded when given opportunities to interfere with safety-relevant tasks. No fine or enforcement action was associated with the publication.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Five Key Findings from First Frontier AI Trends Report
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Five Key Findings from First Frontier AI Trends Report
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published its first Frontier AI Trends Report, setting out five key findings on the current state of frontier AI development. The report examines trends across leading AI systems, covering capability trajectories, safety-relevant behaviours, and emerging risks identified through AISI's evaluation programme. The institute positioned the report as a baseline for tracking how frontier models evolve over time. AISI stated the findings are intended to inform policymakers, researchers, and developers working on the safety and governance of advanced AI systems.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Its Approach to Tackling AI-Generated Child Sexual Abuse Material
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Its Approach to Tackling AI-Generated Child Sexual Abuse Material
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post setting out its approach to tackling AI-generated child sexual abuse material. The post describes how AISI is working to address the threat posed by generative AI tools capable of producing such material. AISI frames the issue as a safety priority requiring technical and policy action. The blog represents the institute's public position on one of the most serious harms associated with AI-generated content.

rows.headline: UK's MHRA Appoints Jason Bonander as Chief Digital and Technology Officer
rows.title: UK's MHRA Appoints Jason Bonander as Chief Digital and Technology Officer
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency appointed Jason Bonander as its new Chief Digital and Technology Officer. Bonander joins MHRA to lead the transformation of the technology systems underpinning the regulation of medicines and medical devices in the United Kingdom. The agency described the hire as bringing top global technology talent to the role. As CDTO, Bonander will oversee digital and technology strategy across MHRA's regulatory functions.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: United Kingdom's Patents Act 1977 Establishes Core Patent Rights and Procedure
rows.title: United Kingdom's Patents Act 1977 Establishes Core Patent Rights and Procedure
rows.summary: On 29 July 1977, the Parliament of the United Kingdom enacted the Patents Act 1977, which received Royal Assent and came into force in stages, with main provisions taking effect on 1 June 1978. The Act replaced the Patents Act 1949 and gave effect to UK obligations under the European Patent Convention 1973 and the Patent Co-operation Treaty 1970. Section 1 sets patentability conditions, requiring novelty, inventive step, and industrial application. Section 60 confers exclusive exploitation rights on patent holders. Sections 39 to 43 govern employee invention ownership and employer compensation obligations. The Intellectual Property Office administers the Act, and the Intellectual Property Act 2014 inserted IPO opinion provisions under sections 74A and 74B.

rows.headline: UK DSIT Secretary Calls for Greater British Control Over AI Infrastructure
rows.title: UK DSIT Secretary Calls for Greater British Control Over AI Infrastructure
rows.summary: On 28 April 2026, Technology Secretary Peter Kyle delivered a speech calling for a decisive shift toward backing British technology companies in AI development. Kyle argued that the United Kingdom must secure greater control and leverage over AI systems to protect national security amid a fractured global order. The speech framed AI as a force reshaping global power, security, and prosperity, and positioned domestic tech investment as a strategic priority. Kyle did not announce specific funding commitments or legislative measures in the address.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK DSIT Backs Ineffable Intelligence to Build Self-Learning Knowledge AI
rows.title: UK DSIT Backs Ineffable Intelligence to Build Self-Learning Knowledge AI
rows.summary: On 27 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology announced backing for Ineffable Intelligence, a company developing self-learning AI designed to generate new knowledge. The investment was made through the government's Sovereign AI initiative, with co-investment from the British Business Bank. The funding is intended to scale a UK-built AI system capable of producing knowledge-generating breakthroughs. DSIT described the initiative as part of broader efforts to develop sovereign AI capabilities within the United Kingdom.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK NCSC and Allies Issue Advisory on China-Linked Covert Network Tactics
rows.title: UK NCSC and Allies Issue Advisory on China-Linked Covert Network Tactics
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre and international partner agencies published a joint advisory on tactics used by China-linked threat actors to conceal malicious cyber activity. The advisory details how these actors operate covert networks to disguise intrusion campaigns. Partner agencies from multiple countries co-signed the guidance. The publication identifies specific attacker techniques linked to efforts to hide the origin and nature of cyber operations attributed to China-linked groups.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Issues Guidance on China-Nexus Compromised-Device Covert Networks
rows.title: UK NCSC Issues Guidance on China-Nexus Compromised-Device Covert Networks
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published updated guidance on defending against covert networks of compromised infrastructure linked to China-nexus threat actors. The guidance details a widespread shift in tactics, techniques and procedures toward the use of compromised devices as operational relay points. It sets out the methods threat actors use to build and exploit these networks and provides technical recommendations for detecting and mitigating associated activity. The publication follows similar advisories from allied cyber authorities on state-linked infrastructure abuse.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Blog Post Asserting Passkeys Exceed Password Security
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Blog Post Asserting Passkeys Exceed Password Security
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post arguing that passkeys are more secure and more usable than traditional password-based authentication methods. The NCSC stated that passkeys are already supported by most modern devices and platforms, lowering the barrier to adoption. The post positioned passkeys as a practical replacement for passwords rather than a theoretical future standard. The NCSC's guidance forms part of its broader effort to improve baseline authentication security across UK organisations and individuals.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on China-Nexus Covert Networks of Compromised Devices
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on China-Nexus Covert Networks of Compromised Devices
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published an executive summary on defending against China-nexus covert networks built from compromised devices. The guidance addresses threat actors who route malicious traffic through hijacked edge devices, including VPNs and remote access infrastructure, to obscure their origin. The NCSC identified these covert networks as a growing operational tool used in state-linked intrusion campaigns. The publication outlines how such networks function, the device categories most commonly targeted, and the indicators organisations can use to detect covert network traffic on their infrastructure.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Urges Consumer Services to Adopt Passkeys as Default Authentication
rows.title: UK NCSC Urges Consumer Services to Adopt Passkeys as Default Authentication
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published guidance recommending that passkeys replace passwords as the default login method for consumer-facing services. The NCSC stated that passkeys are more secure than passwords and offer a simpler user experience. The guidance positions passkeys as the preferred authentication option, arguing that passwords present avoidable security risks. The NCSC called on service providers to make passkeys the default choice rather than an optional alternative, marking a shift in the agency's public authentication recommendations.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on AI Adoption for National Cyber Defence
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on AI Adoption for National Cyber Defence
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post outlining its position on adopting artificial intelligence for cyber defence purposes. The NCSC stated that AI adoption will require time, the development of new capabilities, and careful oversight. The post addresses the operational and governance considerations involved in integrating AI into UK cyber defence. The NCSC did not specify a fixed timeline for implementation or detail particular AI systems under evaluation.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Engineers SilentGlass Device to Block Malicious Display-Link Connections
rows.title: UK NCSC Engineers SilentGlass Device to Block Malicious Display-Link Connections
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre announced SilentGlass, a plug-and-play device designed to block unexpected or malicious HDMI and DisplayPort connections. The NCSC describes SilentGlass as a world-first device it engineered specifically to secure vulnerable display links. The device operates by actively intercepting unauthorised connections at the point of the display interface. SilentGlass requires no software installation and connects directly between a source device and a display. The NCSC published the announcement on its official website alongside technical details of the product.

rows.headline: UK FCA Launches Second Cohort of Eight Firms in AI Live Testing Programme
rows.title: UK FCA Launches Second Cohort of Eight Firms in AI Live Testing Programme
rows.summary: On 21 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority announced at UK FinTech Week that eight firms had commenced testing in the second cohort of its AI Live Testing programme. Participating firms include Barclays, Experian, GoCardless, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), UBS, and Palindrome. Technical partner Advai, a London-based AI assurance specialist, is delivering the programme alongside the FCA. Use cases under testing span agentic payments, credit score insights, anti-money laundering detection, and Know Your Customer processes. Testing operates under existing FCA regulatory frameworks, with no new AI-specific rules introduced. Testing will conclude by end-2026, and the FCA will publish an evaluation report in Q1 2027.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Urges Organisations to Strengthen Defences Against Severe Cyber Threats
rows.title: UK NCSC Urges Organisations to Strengthen Defences Against Severe Cyber Threats
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post calling on organisational leaders to act on the growing severity of the cyber threat landscape. The NCSC described the threat environment as increasingly serious and emphasised the need for collective resilience across UK public and private sectors. The post outlined the importance of senior leadership engagement in cybersecurity decisions. The NCSC directed organisations to its existing guidance and resources as a basis for improving their defensive posture against current and emerging threats.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Update on NHS Cyber Resilience Collaboration and Detection Efforts
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Update on NHS Cyber Resilience Collaboration and Detection Efforts
rows.summary: On 17 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post outlining its work to strengthen cyber resilience across the National Health Service. The NCSC described efforts to reduce cyber risk and improve threat detection across NHS systems. The post highlighted collaboration with NHS organisations as a central element of the approach. The NCSC also noted work aimed at keeping vital health services operational against cyber threats. No specific incidents, statistics, or named partner organisations were cited in the source material.

rows.headline: UK's DSIT Tech Secretary Launches Sovereign AI Fund at Wayve
rows.title: UK's DSIT Tech Secretary Launches Sovereign AI Fund at Wayve
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology Liz Kendall launched the Sovereign AI Fund at a speech held at Wayve. Kendall delivered the announcement in her capacity as Tech Secretary, marking a formal introduction of the fund under the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology. The Sovereign AI Fund represents a named government initiative directed at artificial intelligence development within the United Kingdom. No funding figures, eligibility criteria, or operational details were included in the source materials.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK's DSIT Invites EdTech and AI Firms to Develop Safe AI Tutoring Tools
rows.title: UK's DSIT Invites EdTech and AI Firms to Develop Safe AI Tutoring Tools
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology opened a bidding process for EdTech companies and AI laboratories to develop safe, personalised AI tutoring tools. The initiative targets improved learning outcomes for disadvantaged pupils. Companies and labs are invited to submit bids to build the tools, which are designed to be both safe and tailored to individual learners. The department framed the procurement as part of its broader effort to apply AI to educational equity.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK DSIT Awards First Sovereign AI Grants to Drug Discovery and Supercomputing Firms
rows.title: UK DSIT Awards First Sovereign AI Grants to Drug Discovery and Supercomputing Firms
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology announced the first recipients of funding through the Sovereign AI programme. The programme carries a total budget of GBP 500 million and is designed to support homegrown AI companies. Initial backing went to firms working in drug discovery and cheaper supercomputing, among other areas. DSIT stated the programme aims to drive economic growth and create jobs across the United Kingdom. The announcement marks the first wave of awards under the initiative since it was established.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Finalised Guidance on Storage and Access Technologies
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Finalised Guidance on Storage and Access Technologies
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published its finalised guidance on Storage and Access Technologies (SATs). The guidance sets out the ICO's expectations for how organisations deploy technologies that store or access information on users' devices, including cookies and similar tools. The ICO also released an accompanying update to its online tracking strategy alongside the SATs guidance. The finalised guidance follows an earlier consultation period and represents the ICO's definitive regulatory position on SATs.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns Frontier AI Is Accelerating Vulnerability Discovery by Attackers
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns Frontier AI Is Accelerating Vulnerability Discovery by Attackers
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post warning that frontier AI capabilities are accelerating vulnerability discovery and lowering barriers for cyber attackers. The NCSC stated that offensive actors are gaining AI-enabled advantages in identifying and exploiting weaknesses in digital systems. The centre argued that defenders must raise security baselines to counter this shift in the threat landscape. The post forms part of NCSC's broader effort to communicate how advanced AI models are changing the balance between attack and defence in cyber security.

rows.headline: UK DSIT Writes to Business Leaders on AI Cyber Threats
rows.title: UK DSIT Writes to Business Leaders on AI Cyber Threats
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology published an open letter to business leaders on AI-related cyber threats. The letter was co-signed by DSIT Secretary of State Liz Kendall and Security Minister Dan Jarvis. It addressed the growing threat posed by AI-enabled cyber attacks to UK businesses. The publication forms part of the government's broader effort to alert business leaders to emerging risks in the AI and cybersecurity landscape.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK FCA Consults on Cryptoasset Perimeter Guidance Ahead of October 2027 Regime
rows.title: UK FCA Consults on Cryptoasset Perimeter Guidance Ahead of October 2027 Regime
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published consultation paper CP26-13, seeking feedback on which cryptoasset activities will fall within the scope of the United Kingdom's forthcoming regulatory regime under SI 2026/102. The FCA identifies five regulated activities: issuing qualifying stablecoins, operating trading platforms, dealing and arranging deals in qualifying cryptoassets, safeguarding cryptoassets, and staking. The consultation closes on 3 June 2026, with a final policy statement due in autumn 2026. The authorisation gateway opens on 30 September 2026, and the full regime comes into force in October 2027. Until then, cryptoassets remain largely unregulated in the United Kingdom except for financial promotions and financial crime purposes.

rows.headline: UK NCSC CEO Warns of 'Perfect Storm' as Nation-State Incidents Rise and AI Scales Threats
rows.title: UK NCSC CEO Warns of 'Perfect Storm' as Nation-State Incidents Rise and AI Scales Threats
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, NCSC CEO Dr Richard Horne addressed the CYBERUK conference in Glasgow, setting out the centre's assessment of the current threat landscape. The NCSC reported that the majority of nationally significant incidents it handles now originate directly or indirectly from nation states, with Russia applying battlefield lessons from Ukraine to cyber operations. The authority stated that frontier AI is rapidly enabling vulnerability discovery and exploitation at scale. The NCSC also expanded its definition of cyber security to encompass robotics, autonomous systems, and body-integrated technology, describing these as far beyond how the discipline was understood a decade ago.

rows.headline: UK's MHRA Sets Out Strategy for Seizing AI and Technology Opportunities
rows.title: UK's MHRA Sets Out Strategy for Seizing AI and Technology Opportunities
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency published a statement by Dame Jennifer Dixon on how the agency intends to capture growing opportunities presented by artificial intelligence and technology. The piece outlines the MHRA's forward-looking position on integrating AI and technology within its regulatory remit. No specific policy changes, enforcement actions, or binding guidance were announced alongside the statement. The publication reflects the agency's broader interest in positioning itself to respond to technological developments in the medicines and healthcare products sector.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: UK FCA Publishes Open Finance Roadmap Targeting Regulatory Framework by End of 2027
rows.title: UK FCA Publishes Open Finance Roadmap Targeting Regulatory Framework by End of 2027
rows.summary: On 14 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published an open finance roadmap setting out its programme to enable consumers and businesses to share financial data securely across financial services providers. The roadmap identifies SME credit access and mortgage management as priority areas for early exploration. The FCA will consult on a long-term regulatory framework for open banking before end of 2026, separate from the broader open finance framework it must develop with HM Treasury by end of 2027. Industry and consumer engagement will run through 2026 via the FCA's Smart Data Accelerator and PRISM Taskforce. Open banking currently has approximately 17 million users in the United Kingdom.

rows.headline: UK DSIT Proposes Criminal Sanctions for Incest and Age-Disguise Pornography
rows.title: UK DSIT Proposes Criminal Sanctions for Incest and Age-Disguise Pornography
rows.summary: On 10 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology announced proposed legislation to criminalise the possession and publication of pornography depicting incest or adults simulating minors. The measures would also introduce personal liability for technology executives whose platforms host such material. DSIT stated the changes form part of broader efforts to address harmful online content. The proposals were published alongside the government's existing Online Safety Act framework, extending criminal reach to categories of material not previously covered by specific possession offences.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK's MHRA Secures GBP 36 Million to Expand AI Airlock Programme
rows.title: UK's MHRA Secures GBP 36 Million to Expand AI Airlock Programme
rows.summary: On 8 April 2026, the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency announced GBP 36 million in funding to expand its AI Airlock programme over three years. The AI Airlock is a regulatory sandbox that allows developers to test AI-powered medical devices under MHRA supervision before market approval. The multi-year funding will increase the programme's capacity to assess AI products entering the UK healthcare market. MHRA first launched the AI Airlock as a pilot initiative to build regulatory frameworks for AI in medicine.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Is Exploiting Vulnerable Edge Devices for Cyber Operations
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Is Exploiting Vulnerable Edge Devices for Cyber Operations
rows.summary: On 7 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published an advisory attributing a campaign of router and edge-device exploitation to APT28, a cyber threat group linked to Russian military intelligence. The advisory states that APT28 actors hijacked vulnerable routers to support malicious cyber operations. The NCSC identified the group as operating on behalf of Russia's GRU military intelligence service. The advisory details how compromised edge devices were used as infrastructure to conduct and obscure the group's activities.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Exploits Routers to Conduct DNS Hijacking
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Exploits Routers to Conduct DNS Hijacking
rows.summary: On 7 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published an alert warning that Russian cyber actor APT28 is exploiting vulnerable routers to hijack DNS. The technique enables adversary-in-the-middle attacks against targeted networks. Attackers use this access to intercept traffic and steal passwords and authentication tokens. The NCSC attributed the activity to APT28, a threat actor widely linked to Russian military intelligence. The advisory urges router operators to review device security to reduce exposure to the identified attack methods.

rows.headline: UK MHRA and US FDA Deepen Medical Device Regulatory Cooperation
rows.title: UK MHRA and US FDA Deepen Medical Device Regulatory Cooperation
rows.summary: On 2 April 2026, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency and the US Food and Drug Administration announced strengthened cooperation on medical device regulation. The agreement builds on an existing wider pharmaceutical partnership between the two agencies. Both regulators stated the collaboration aims to support faster patient access to safe and innovative medical technologies in the United Kingdom and the United States. The MHRA described the move as an extension of ongoing transatlantic regulatory alignment efforts covering the medical devices sector specifically.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: UK CMA Announces Remedies Package for Business Software and Cloud Services Markets
rows.title: UK CMA Announces Remedies Package for Business Software and Cloud Services Markets
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the UK Competition and Markets Authority announced a package of actions targeting business software and cloud services markets. The CMA stated the measures aim to create greater choice for UK businesses and public sector organisations. The announcement follows the authority's review of competition conditions in these sectors. The CMA identified concerns about market dynamics at a time of significant advances in artificial intelligence. The package of actions covers both business software and cloud services, though specific remedies and timelines were not detailed in the source material.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns High-Risk Individuals of Messaging App Targeting Threat
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns High-Risk Individuals of Messaging App Targeting Threat
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre issued a warning about targeted attacks against messaging apps affecting high-risk individuals. The NCSC published specific actions for people it considers at elevated risk from such attacks. The guidance addresses threats directed at messaging applications used by individuals who may be subject to state-sponsored or other sophisticated targeting. The NCSC urged those in at-risk categories to review and apply the recommended steps to reduce their exposure.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Revised Automated Decision-Making Guidance Under DUA Act 2025
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Revised Automated Decision-Making Guidance Under DUA Act 2025
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office published updated guidance on automated decision-making and profiling, reflecting amendments to the UK GDPR introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025. The revised guidance consolidates obligations under the newly inserted articles 22A to 22D, which govern solely automated decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects on individuals. New sections address when restrictions on ADM apply, required conditions, mandatory safeguards, and individual rights. The ICO states the guidance will inform a forthcoming statutory code of practice on artificial intelligence and ADM. Previous guidance remains available as a PDF pending finalisation of a concurrent consultation.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns Cyber Defenders to Prepare for Frontier AI Threats
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns Cyber Defenders to Prepare for Frontier AI Threats
rows.summary: On 30 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post warning that frontier AI is reshaping the cyber threat landscape. The NCSC stated that adversaries are already exploring frontier AI capabilities to enhance attacks, and that defenders must understand these developments to stay ahead. The post identified frontier AI as a material risk to existing cyber defences. The NCSC called on cyber defenders to monitor how frontier AI evolves and to factor its capabilities into threat assessments and defensive planning.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Cloud-Hosted SCADA Guidance for Operational Technology Organisations
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Cloud-Hosted SCADA Guidance for Operational Technology Organisations
rows.summary: On 24 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) published guidance on migrating SCADA systems to cloud environments. The guidance targets operational technology organisations, including critical national infrastructure operators. It identifies three assessment areas organisations must address before migration: business drivers and cloud opportunities, organisational readiness, and technology and cloud solution suitability. The NCSC noted a clear industry shift toward cloud adoption for industrial applications, where such adoption was previously dismissed due to perceived risk. The guidance was developed with input from industry bodies, CNI operators, and SCADA vendors, and is intended for use alongside existing NCSC cloud security guidance.

rows.headline: UK ICO Sets Out Data Protection Framework for Police Facial Recognition Use
rows.title: UK ICO Sets Out Data Protection Framework for Police Facial Recognition Use
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published a blog post explaining why data protection sits at the centre of responsible police use of facial recognition technology. The ICO outlined its position on how data protection law applies when law enforcement agencies deploy facial recognition systems. The post addressed the conditions under which such technology can be used lawfully and the safeguards required to protect individuals' rights. The ICO stated that strong data protection standards and effective policing are not mutually exclusive goals.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Police Scotland GBP 66,000 and Issues Reprimand for Data Mishandling
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Police Scotland GBP 66,000 and Issues Reprimand for Data Mishandling
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined Police Scotland GBP 66,000 and issued a formal reprimand for serious failures in the handling of sensitive personal information. The ICO determined that Police Scotland mishandled personal data in a manner serious enough to warrant both a financial penalty and a reprimand. The dual sanction reflects the ICO's assessment of the severity of the data protection failures involved.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Open Letter Urging Platforms to Strengthen Children's Age Assurance
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Open Letter Urging Platforms to Strengthen Children's Age Assurance
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published an open letter to social media and video-sharing platforms operating in the United Kingdom. The letter calls on platforms to strengthen age assurance measures to prevent young children from accessing services not designed for them. The ICO directed the letter at firms subject to UK data protection law, urging them to ensure robust age checks are in place. The open letter forms part of the ICO's broader effort to enforce children's data protections under existing UK rules.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Birmingham Pendant Alarm Firm GBP 100,000 for Unsolicited TPS Calls
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Birmingham Pendant Alarm Firm GBP 100,000 for Unsolicited TPS Calls
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined a Birmingham-based pendant alarm company GBP 100,000 for making unsolicited marketing calls. The company placed over 260,000 calls to numbers registered on the Telephone Preference Service, breaching direct marketing rules. Registering with the TPS signals that consumers do not wish to receive such calls. The ICO determined the calls constituted a clear violation of the applicable legal framework governing electronic marketing communications.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Jobseeker Guidance on Automated Recruitment Decisions
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Jobseeker Guidance on Automated Recruitment Decisions
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published guidance explaining what jobseekers should know about automated decision-making in recruitment. The guidance sets out that organisations using automated tools to screen or rank candidates must inform applicants when such systems are used. Jobseekers retain the right to request human review of automated decisions that significantly affect them. The ICO stated that employers must be transparent about the logic involved in automated processes and cannot rely solely on automated outcomes without providing meaningful recourse. The publication forms part of the ICO's broader work on artificial intelligence and data protection in employment contexts.

rows.headline: UK ICO and Ofcom Publish Joint Statement on Age Assurance Regulatory Overlap
rows.title: UK ICO and Ofcom Publish Joint Statement on Age Assurance Regulatory Overlap
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office and Ofcom published a joint statement identifying the main areas of interaction between online safety law and data protection law as they relate to age assurance. The statement sets out where the two regulatory frameworks overlap and how each authority's remit applies. The ICO leads on data protection obligations, while Ofcom holds responsibility for online safety duties. The joint statement aims to clarify how the two regimes interact for services that deploy age assurance measures.

rows.headline: UK FCA, ICO, SRA and ASA Form Joint Taskforce on Motor Finance Claims
rows.title: UK FCA, ICO, SRA and ASA Form Joint Taskforce on Motor Finance Claims
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority, Information Commissioner's Office, Solicitors Regulation Authority and Advertising Standards Authority announced a joint taskforce targeting poor practice in motor finance claims handling. The four regulators agreed to coordinate their efforts against claims management companies and law firms identified as handling motor finance complaints poorly. The FCA leads financial conduct oversight, the SRA covers solicitor conduct, the ICO addresses data protection concerns, and the ASA focuses on advertising standards. The taskforce will combine each regulator's existing powers to address misconduct across the sector.

rows.headline: UK ICO Calls on Businesses to Review Automated Decision-Making in Recruitment
rows.title: UK ICO Calls on Businesses to Review Automated Decision-Making in Recruitment
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published guidance calling on businesses to review their use of automated decision-making in recruitment. The ICO stated that automated decisions can streamline hiring but must include appropriate protections for candidates. The regulator directed businesses to assess whether their current processes meet the required legal safeguards under data protection law. The ICO did not announce fines or enforcement action, framing the publication as a proactive call to review existing practices.

rows.headline: UK ICO and Global Authorities Publish Joint Statement on AI-Generated Imagery Privacy Risks
rows.title: UK ICO and Global Authorities Publish Joint Statement on AI-Generated Imagery Privacy Risks
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office and data protection authorities from multiple jurisdictions published a joint statement on the privacy risks of AI-generated imagery. The statement sets out the authorities' collective position on how AI image generation technologies interact with data protection obligations. The ICO joined regulators from across the globe in issuing the document, marking a coordinated international effort to address privacy concerns arising from AI-generated visual content.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Reddit GBP 14.47 Million for Children's Privacy Failures
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Reddit GBP 14.47 Million for Children's Privacy Failures
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office issued Reddit, Inc. a GBP 14.47 million fine for unlawfully using children's personal information. The ICO's investigation found that Reddit processed children's data in breach of UK data protection law. The regulator determined Reddit failed to implement adequate measures to prevent children from accessing content and having their personal information used without a lawful basis. The fine represents one of the ICO's more significant enforcement actions against a major social media platform for child privacy violations.

rows.headline: UK ICO Reports Progress on Economic Growth Commitments to Government
rows.title: UK ICO Reports Progress on Economic Growth Commitments to Government
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published an update to government on its economic growth commitments. In January 2025, the ICO had responded to a government call for proposals aimed at boosting the UK economy. The February update sets out progress made against those commitments. The ICO framed its regulatory approach as a contributor to economic growth, reporting on actions taken since the original January 2025 submission.

rows.headline: UK ICO Marks Commencement of the Data Use and Access Act
rows.title: UK ICO Marks Commencement of the Data Use and Access Act
rows.summary: On 5 February 2026, the next phase of the Data (Use and Access) Act implementation commenced in the United Kingdom. The Information Commissioner's Office issued a statement marking the development. The DUAA represents a significant update to the UK's data regulation framework, succeeding prior legislation. The ICO's statement acknowledged the commencement date and signalled the authority's role in the Act's ongoing implementation. No enforcement actions, fines, or specific operational guidance were announced alongside the statement.

rows.headline: UK ICO Opens Formal Investigations into X and X.AI Over Grok Data Processing
rows.title: UK ICO Opens Formal Investigations into X and X.AI Over Grok Data Processing
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office opened formal investigations into X Internet Unlimited Company and X.AI LLC. Both investigations cover the companies' processing of personal data in connection with the Grok artificial intelligence system. The ICO also cited Grok's potential to produce harmful sexualised image and video content as part of its inquiry scope. The investigations are separate actions targeting the two entities individually.

rows.headline: UK ICO Wins Court of Appeal Ruling Against DSG Retail Limited
rows.title: UK ICO Wins Court of Appeal Ruling Against DSG Retail Limited
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office succeeded in its Court of Appeal case against DSG Retail Limited. The Court of Appeal overturned the earlier Upper Tribunal decision, ruling in favour of the ICO. The case concerned DSG Retail Limited, a major UK electronics retailer. The ICO had originally taken action against DSG and subsequently appealed after the Upper Tribunal ruled against it. The Court of Appeal's decision restores the ICO's original position in the matter.

rows.headline: UK ICO Responds to MPs' February Letter Regarding Tattle Life Platform
rows.title: UK ICO Responds to MPs' February Letter Regarding Tattle Life Platform
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published its response to a letter sent by MPs on 9 February 2026 concerning Tattle Life. The ICO addressed the concerns raised by the MPs about the platform. Tattle Life is a website that has been the subject of parliamentary attention in the United Kingdom. The ICO's response sets out the regulator's position on the matters raised in the MPs' correspondence.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Imgur Owner MediaLab for Children's Privacy Violations
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Imgur Owner MediaLab for Children's Privacy Violations
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined MediaLab, the owner of image-sharing platform Imgur, for unlawfully processing children's personal data. The ICO's investigation found that MediaLab collected and used data belonging to child users without a lawful basis under UK data protection law. The regulator concluded that MediaLab failed to implement adequate safeguards to prevent children from being exposed to privacy risks on the platform. The ICO published its findings alongside the penalty notice on its official media centre.

rows.headline: UK ICO Reprimands Staines Health Group for Excessive Medical Disclosure to Insurer
rows.title: UK ICO Reprimands Staines Health Group for Excessive Medical Disclosure to Insurer
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office reprimanded Staines Health Group, a GP surgery, for sending excessive medical history about a terminally ill patient to the patient's insurance company. The ICO found that the volume of medical detail disclosed exceeded what was necessary for the insurance purpose. The reprimand marks a formal finding against the surgery for failing to apply data minimisation principles when responding to the insurer's request. No financial penalty was issued alongside the reprimand.

rows.headline: UK ICO Secures Guilty Verdicts Against Eight Men in Largest Nuisance Call Investigation
rows.title: UK ICO Secures Guilty Verdicts Against Eight Men in Largest Nuisance Call Investigation
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office secured guilty verdicts against eight men following its largest ever nuisance call investigation. The men were convicted for unlawfully accessing and obtaining personal information from vehicle repair garages. That data was then used to generate potential leads for personal injury claims. The ICO brought the prosecution under laws prohibiting the unlawful obtaining of personal data. The case represents the largest criminal investigation the ICO has undertaken related to nuisance call activity and data theft from the automotive repair sector.

rows.headline: John Edwards speaks at IAPP UK Intensive 2026
rows.title: John Edwards speaks at IAPP UK Intensive 2026
rows.summary: The Information Commissioner gives the keynote speech.

rows.headline: UK ICO Signs Memorandum of Understanding With His Majesty's Government on Data Protection Standards
rows.title: UK ICO Signs Memorandum of Understanding With His Majesty's Government on Data Protection Standards
rows.summary: On 8 January 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office and His Majesty's Government signed a Memorandum of Understanding committing the government to raising data protection standards. The MOU formalises the relationship between the ICO and HM Government, setting out shared expectations for how government bodies will handle personal data. The agreement marks a formal commitment by government to meet data protection obligations and work cooperatively with the ICO. The ICO published a statement about the signing on 15 January 2026.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Two Companies GBP 225,000 for Unlawful Unsolicited Marketing Messages
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Two Companies GBP 225,000 for Unlawful Unsolicited Marketing Messages
rows.summary: On 15 January 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined two companies a combined GBP 225,000 for sending millions of unsolicited marketing messages in breach of the law. The ICO identified the messages as nuisance marketing communications that violated applicable regulations. Two separate companies received fines as part of the same enforcement action. The total penalty of GBP 225,000 was shared across both firms. The ICO did not disclose the individual fine amounts attributed to each company in its published notice.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on International Data Transfers
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on International Data Transfers
rows.summary: On 15 January 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published updated guidance on international data transfers. The guidance covers the rules organisations must follow when transferring personal data outside the United Kingdom. The ICO indicated the update reflects developments in UK data transfer law since earlier versions were issued. Organisations conducting cross-border transfers are subject to the requirements set out in the updated document. The guidance is available on the ICO website.

rows.headline: UK ICO Report Warns Agentic AI Shopping Tools Could Arrive Within Five Years
rows.title: UK ICO Report Warns Agentic AI Shopping Tools Could Arrive Within Five Years
rows.summary: On 15 January 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published a report examining how agentic artificial intelligence could transform consumer shopping. The report describes a future in which personal AI agents autonomously browse, select, and purchase goods on behalf of individuals. The ICO projects these tools could become common within five years. The report focuses on the rise of agentic AI, in which systems act independently to complete tasks rather than simply responding to prompts. The ICO did not issue fines, enforcement notices, or regulatory guidance alongside the publication.

rows.headline: UK ICO Issues Statement on Grok AI Data Use by X
rows.title: UK ICO Issues Statement on Grok AI Data Use by X
rows.summary: On 15 January 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office issued a statement addressing the use of personal data by Grok AI on the X platform. The ICO stated that people have a right to use social media with confidence that their personal data is handled lawfully and with respect. The office indicated it was examining the matter. No enforcement action, fine, or formal investigation was announced in the statement.

rows.headline: Statement on the introduction of the Cyber Security and Resilience (Network and Information Systems) Bill in the House of Commons
rows.title: Statement on the introduction of the Cyber Security and Resilience (Network and Information Systems) Bill in the House of Commons
rows.summary: Statement on the introduction of the Cyber Security and Resilience (Network and Information Systems) Bill in the House of Commons

rows.headline: ICO to investigate Prospect data breach with Guernsey, Jersey and Isle of Man counterparts
rows.title: ICO to investigate Prospect data breach with Guernsey, Jersey and Isle of Man counterparts
rows.summary: ICO to investigate Prospect data breach with Guernsey, Jersey and Isle of Man counterparts

rows.headline: Password manager provider fined £1.2m by ICO for data breach
rows.title: Password manager provider fined £1.2m by ICO for data breach
rows.summary: The Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) has fined password manager provider LastPass UK Ltd £1.2 million following a 2022 data breach that compromised the personal information of up to 1.6 million of its UK users.

rows.headline: Children’s online privacy in mobile games under spotlight
rows.title: Children’s online privacy in mobile games under spotlight
rows.summary: We are today announcing that we will be scrutinising how popular mobile games played by children in the UK protect their online privacy.

rows.headline: Regulator holds public bodies to account by cracking down on FOI failures
rows.title: Regulator holds public bodies to account by cracking down on FOI failures
rows.summary: Regulator holds public bodies to account by cracking down on FOI failures

rows.headline: Post Office reprimanded over Horizon IT scandal victims' ‘entirely preventable’ data breach
rows.title: Post Office reprimanded over Horizon IT scandal victims' ‘entirely preventable’ data breach
rows.summary: Post Office reprimanded over Horizon IT scandal victims' ‘entirely preventable’ data breach

rows.headline: ICO action secures increased cookie compliance, giving millions stronger control over their personal information online
rows.title: ICO action secures increased cookie compliance, giving millions stronger control over their personal information online
rows.summary: ICO action secures increased cookie compliance, giving millions stronger control over their personal information online

rows.headline: Statement on the National Physical Laboratory facial recognition technology report
rows.title: Statement on the National Physical Laboratory facial recognition technology report

rows.headline: Jointly Prepared Statement from the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) and  Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) on Targeted Support and Direct Marketing
rows.title: Jointly Prepared Statement from the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) and  Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) on Targeted Support and Direct Marketing

rows.headline: John Edwards' opening speech at DPPC 2025
rows.title: John Edwards' opening speech at DPPC 2025
rows.summary: Kia ora koutou, hello and welcome to the ICO’s annual conference, the Data Protection Practitioners’ Conference.

rows.headline: £200,000 fine for sole trader who sent nearly one million spam texts
rows.title: £200,000 fine for sole trader who sent nearly one million spam texts
rows.summary: £200,000 fine for sole trader who sent nearly one million spam texts

rows.headline: ICO call for views on enforcement procedural guidance
rows.title: ICO call for views on enforcement procedural guidance
rows.summary: ICO call for views on enforcement procedural guidance

rows.headline: Crack down on police data delays
rows.title: Crack down on police data delays
rows.summary: Crack down on police data delays

rows.headline: Capita fined £14m for data breach affecting over 6m people
rows.title: Capita fined £14m for data breach affecting over 6m people
rows.summary: Capita fined £14m for data breach affecting over 6m people

rows.headline: UK Upper Tribunal hands down judgment on Clearview AI Inc
rows.title: UK Upper Tribunal hands down judgment on Clearview AI Inc
rows.summary: UK Upper Tribunal hands down judgment on Clearview AI Inc

rows.headline: ICO statement on changes to Meta advertising model
rows.title: ICO statement on changes to Meta advertising model
rows.summary: Following the ICO's engagement with Meta about how it uses personal information for its advertising model, we welcome Meta's decision to ask users for consent to use their personal information to target them with ads.

rows.headline: Statement: Update on Imgur investigation
rows.title: Statement: Update on Imgur investigation
rows.summary: Statement: Update on Imgur investigation

rows.headline: Insider threat of students leading to increasing number of cyber attacks in schools
rows.title: Insider threat of students leading to increasing number of cyber attacks in schools
rows.summary: Over half of school insider cyber attacks caused by students

rows.headline: Information Commissioner’s Office shares cyber security tips for small businesses
rows.title: Information Commissioner’s Office shares cyber security tips for small businesses
rows.summary: With cyber attacks regularly in the news, we are reminding businesses to check they have the appropriate security measures in place to protect personal information.

rows.headline: Care home director found guilty of ignoring request for personal information
rows.title: Care home director found guilty of ignoring request for personal information
rows.summary: The director of a care home in Bridlington, Yorkshire, has been fined for refusing to respond to a request for a resident’s personal information – known as a subject access request (SAR).

rows.headline: Bristol City Council issued with enforcement notice over failures to respond to data requests
rows.title: Bristol City Council issued with enforcement notice over failures to respond to data requests
rows.summary: We have issued an enforcement notice to Bristol City Council (BCC) for failing to comply with its legal obligations to respond to people who asked for the personal information the council held on them – known as a subject access request (SAR).

rows.headline: Warning over robo calls as energy firms fined half a million pounds for unlawful marketing calls
rows.title: Warning over robo calls as energy firms fined half a million pounds for unlawful marketing calls
rows.summary:
We are warning the public to be on their guard against unlawful robo calls.  

It comes as we have fined two energy companies a total of £550,000 for making automated marketing calls.

rows.headline: ICO launches consultations for Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 amendments
rows.title: ICO launches consultations for Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 amendments
rows.summary: ICO launches consultations for Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 amendments

rows.headline: Guilty: Police conduct investigator fined after unlawfully obtaining sensitive case details
rows.title: Guilty: Police conduct investigator fined after unlawfully obtaining sensitive case details
rows.summary: Guilty: Police conduct investigator fined after unlawfully obtaining sensitive case details

rows.headline: ICO steps up scrutiny of police use of facial recognition technology with audit of South Wales Police and Gwent Police
rows.title: ICO steps up scrutiny of police use of facial recognition technology with audit of South Wales Police and Gwent Police
rows.summary: ICO steps up scrutiny of police use of facial recognition technology with audit of South Wales Police and Gwent Police

rows.headline: South Yorkshire Police reprimanded following deletion of body-worn video evidence
rows.title: South Yorkshire Police reprimanded following deletion of body-worn video evidence
rows.summary: We have reprimanded South Yorkshire Police (SYP) after the force deleted over 96,000 pieces of body-worn video (BWV) evidence.

rows.headline: ICO clarifies how data protection law applies to facial recognition technology
rows.title: ICO clarifies how data protection law applies to facial recognition technology
rows.summary: ICO clarifies how data protection law applies to facial recognition technology

rows.headline: UK - IPO Creative and AI Sectors Kick Off Next Steps in Finding Solutions to AI and Copyright
rows.title: UK - IPO Creative and AI Sectors Kick Off Next Steps in Finding Solutions to AI and Copyright
rows.summary: Representatives of both sectors in newly formed expert working groups on AI and copyright.

rows.headline: Explaining our approach to the MoD data breach
rows.title: Explaining our approach to the MoD data breach

rows.headline: Statement on Supreme Court judgment on Freedom of Information Act appeal
rows.title: Statement on Supreme Court judgment on Freedom of Information Act appeal
rows.summary: The Supreme Court handed down its judgement on 23 July 2025, which dismissed the ICO's appeal.

rows.headline: ICO opens door to privacy-first advertising models with proposed new enforcement approach
rows.title: ICO opens door to privacy-first advertising models with proposed new enforcement approach
rows.summary: We are calling for views on a new enforcement approach that could unlock privacy-preserving alternatives to the dominant adtech business model.

rows.headline: ICO welcomes Tribunal ruling on preliminary issue raised by TikTok in its appeal of 2023 penalty
rows.title: ICO welcomes Tribunal ruling on preliminary issue raised by TikTok in its appeal of 2023 penalty
rows.summary: ICO welcomes Tribunal ruling on preliminary issue raised by TikTok in its appeal of 2023 penalty

rows.headline: Charity fined following destruction of irreplaceable personal records
rows.title: Charity fined following destruction of irreplaceable personal records
rows.summary: We have fined Scottish charity Birthlink £18,000 after it destroyed approximately 4,800 personal records, up to ten percent of which may be irreplaceable.

rows.headline: ICO statement in response to 2022 MoD data breach
rows.title: ICO statement in response to 2022 MoD data breach
rows.summary: We have been carefully considering the circumstances of the breach throughout, supporting the MoD's own investigation.

rows.headline: New guidance on disclosing documents to the public
rows.title: New guidance on disclosing documents to the public
rows.summary: We have today published new guidance to help organisations disclose documents securely.

rows.headline: ICO Places PECR Guide Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act Coming into Force
rows.title: ICO Places PECR Guide Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations under review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force in the United Kingdom.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations is subject to review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into law on that date. The ICO notes that the guidance may be subject to change and directs organisations to its Plans for New and Updated Guidance page for information on which guidance will be updated and when.

The ICO states that the guide is intended for organisations that send electronic marketing messages by phone, fax, email or text, use cookies, or provide electronic communication services to the public. The guide explains how to apply the Regulations through practical examples and answers to frequently asked questions. The ICO confirms that, pending the review, the existing guidance remains the current reference point for compliance with the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations.

The ICO's announcement reflects the broader legislative change introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which the authority identifies as the basis for placing existing PECR guidance under review. The ICO has not specified a timetable for the completion of that review but indicates that updates will be communicated through its dedicated guidance plans page. The ICO has not announced any amendments to the substantive obligations under the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations in connection with this review notice.

rows.headline: ICO Places Children's Code Guidance Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Coming into Force
rows.title: ICO Places Children's Code Guidance Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Children's code guidance and associated resources under formal review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on 19 June 2025, with the ICO noting that existing materials may be subject to change.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Children's code guidance, which covers the age-appropriate design code of practice for online services likely to be accessed by children, is under review as a consequence of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 entering into law on that date. The ICO notes that no financial penalty is associated with this announcement.

The ICO states that the Children's code sets out fifteen standards with which providers of information society services likely to be accessed by children, including apps, online games, and social media sites, are required to conform. The ICO notes that the guidance applies to organisations of all sizes and across all sectors. The ICO states that resources available under the existing framework include a self-assessment risk toolkit enabling organisations to assess how both the UK GDPR and the Children's code apply to their digital services, Data Protection Impact Assessment templates, and sector-specific guidance covering edtech, the digital news industry, and games designers.

The ICO further states that its published Children's Code Strategy, together with a progress update issued in March 2025, sets out the authority's enforcement priorities and actions taken to date in relation to children's privacy online. The ICO's Commissioner's opinion on age assurance details how the Commissioner expects businesses to meet the code's age-appropriate application standard and sets out expectations for age assurance data protection compliance. The ICO also notes that it has co-signed a joint statement on a common international approach to age assurance, establishing shared principles across signatory regulators. The ICO states that its plans for new and updated guidance page is the reference point for information on which guidance will be updated and the timeline for those updates.

rows.headline: ICO Publishes AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit Version 1.1
rows.title: ICO Publishes AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit Version 1.1
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published version 1.1 of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, providing a practical spreadsheet-based resource for organisations processing personal data through AI systems under the UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released an updated iteration of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, available for download as a Microsoft Excel file. The publication coincides with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its broader AI and data protection guidance is currently under review in light of that legislation and may be subject to change, with updates to be signalled via the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the toolkit is designed to provide practical support to organisations seeking to reduce risks to individuals' rights and freedoms arising from their own AI systems. The resource is framed as supplementary material sitting alongside the ICO's existing guidance on AI and data protection, which the ICO states addresses obligations under the UK GDPR including requirements relating to lawful basis, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and accountability (Art. 5, Art. 6, Art. 24 UK GDPR). The ICO presents the toolkit as a means of helping organisations work through data protection risk considerations in a structured, documented format consistent with the accountability principle.

The ICO confirms it is no longer seeking case study submissions from organisations in connection with the toolkit, contrary to an earlier call for contributions referenced in accompanying guidance materials. The toolkit is offered as a downloadable spreadsheet compatible with Microsoft Excel or equivalent programmes, and the ICO notes that organisations on networks that restrict access to video platforms such as Vimeo may be unable to view associated instructional video content and are advised to use an alternative device. The ICO states that no specific compliance deadline or enforcement context is attached to the toolkit's publication.

rows.headline: ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on AI and Data Protection, Adding New Chapters on Fairness and Transparency
rows.title: ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on AI and Data Protection, Adding New Chapters on Fairness and Transparency
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published updated guidance on artificial intelligence and data protection under the UK GDPR, restructuring existing content and introducing new chapters on fairness, transparency, and lawfulness in AI systems.

On 15 March 2023, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released a revised version of its Guidance on AI and Data Protection, with the document remaining subject to further review following the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025. The ICO states that the guidance carries no financial penalty and imposes no fines; it is a compliance guidance instrument addressing obligations under the UK GDPR.

The ICO states that the update was issued in response to requests from UK industry for clarification on fairness requirements in AI, and that it delivers on a commitment under the ICO25 strategic plan to help organisations adopt new technologies whilst protecting individuals and vulnerable groups. The ICO finds that the guidance has been restructured around data protection principles, which provides a more editorially coherent and operationally sustainable framework given the pace of technological development.

The ICO notes that the revised guidance introduces several new and reorganised chapters. The ICO states that a standalone chapter on transparency sets out how the transparency principle (Art. 5(1)(a) UK GDPR) applies to AI, with detailed explainability content retained within the ICO's separate Explaining Decisions Made with AI product. The ICO notes that a new chapter on lawfulness includes added content on AI-generated inferences, affinity groups, and special category data (Art. 9 UK GDPR). The ICO states that it also introduces a dedicated fairness chapter covering the distinction between data protection fairness, algorithmic fairness, bias, and discrimination, as well as technical approaches to bias mitigation and the relationship between automated decision-making safeguards and fairness considerations (Art. 22 UK GDPR). The ICO notes that a new Annex A addresses fairness across the full AI lifecycle, from problem formulation through to decommissioning, identifying sources of bias arising from underlying assumptions, target variable selection, and reliance on quantifiable proxies. The ICO states that statistical accuracy content, previously located within the lawfulness and fairness chapter, has been relocated to a dedicated accuracy chapter reflecting its closer alignment with the accuracy principle (Art. 5(1)(d) UK GDPR). The ICO notes the guidance will continue to be updated to reflect forthcoming regulatory reform and the UK government's pro-innovation approach to AI regulation.

rows.headline: ICO Issues Guidance on Personal Data Use in Political Campaigning Under UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR
rows.title: ICO Issues Guidance on Personal Data Use in Political Campaigning Under UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published guidance on the use of personal data in political campaigning, setting out compliance expectations for controllers under the UK General Data Protection Regulation, the Data Protection Act 2018 and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (PECR).

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published its finalised guidance for the use of personal data in political campaigning, following a consultation launched in October 2019 on a draft framework code of practice. The ICO notes that the guidance is currently under review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into force on the same date, and may be subject to change. The ICO states that no fines are associated with this guidance publication.

The ICO states that the guidance is directed at controllers processing personal data for political campaigning purposes, defined as activity in support of, or against, a political party, a referendum campaign, a candidate standing for election, or the success or failure of a recall petition. The ICO confirms this includes registered political parties, candidates, referendum campaigners, non-party campaigners and recall petition campaigners as defined under the Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000 (ss. 23, 88, 105), the Representation of the People Act 1983 (s. 118A), and the Recall of MPs Act 2015 (Schedule 3, Part 5). The ICO notes that the guidance does not apply to internal party processes such as leadership elections.

The ICO finds that political campaigning has become increasingly sophisticated through the use of digital technologies and commercial marketing techniques, and that the often invisible nature of these techniques risks undermining public trust in the democratic process. The ICO states that the guidance does not introduce any new obligations or responsibilities beyond those already imposed by existing data protection and electronic marketing law, but is intended to provide clarity and practical advice to ensure that all organisations involved in political campaigning handle personal data in a manner compliant with the UK GDPR, the DPA 2018 and PECR, regardless of the method of communication or future technological developments.

rows.headline: ICO Publishes Sample DPIA for Children's Mobile Gaming Apps Under Age-Appropriate Design Code
rows.title: ICO Publishes Sample DPIA for Children's Mobile Gaming Apps Under Age-Appropriate Design Code
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published a sample Data Protection Impact Assessment for a fictional mobile gaming application aimed at children in the United Kingdom, intended to illustrate good practice compliance with Standard 2 of the Children's Code (Age-Appropriate Design Code) under UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published the sample DPIA, coinciding with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its guidance, including this resource, is under review and may be subject to change as a result of that legislation, with updates to be tracked on the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the sample DPIA is adapted from its own DPIA template and is intended to help companies developing mobile gaming apps for children in the UK understand and apply Standard 2 of the Children's Code, which the ICO notes requires Information Society Services likely to be accessed by children under the age of 18 to carry out a DPIA (Art. 35 UK GDPR). The document uses a fictional product, the Cooking Numbers mobile app, developed by the controller named as The Mobile Game Company, based on a product specification commissioned from Fundamentally Games for demonstration purposes only. The ICO confirms that the fictional product will not be developed into an actual game.

The ICO states that the sample follows the process set out in its DPIA guidance and the Children's Code, and recommends reading it alongside the code's dedicated DPIA guidance and the criteria for an acceptable DPIA established in European guidelines. The ICO further notes that its design guidance, which includes gaming-specific worked examples of data privacy moments and age-appropriate mindsets developed from the Fundamentally Games product specification, is available to assist practitioners in identifying what data is being processed and any associated risks. The authority invites feedback and recommendations for improvements to the sample DPIA by email.

rows.headline: New guidance to help smart product manufacturers get data protection right
rows.title: New guidance to help smart product manufacturers get data protection right
rows.summary: We are calling on all manufacturers and developers of smart products to prioritise people’s privacy with new guidance published today (16 June).

rows.headline: 23andMe fined £2.31 million for failing to protect UK users’ genetic data
rows.title: 23andMe fined £2.31 million for failing to protect UK users’ genetic data
rows.summary: 23andMe fined £2.31 million for failing to protect UK users’ genetic data

rows.headline: Tech, Trust and Teamwork: How the FCA & ICO are Helping Innovation Take Off
rows.title: Tech, Trust and Teamwork: How the FCA & ICO are Helping Innovation Take Off
rows.summary: From AI to digital ID, data-driven technologies are transforming financial services. And how we harness these technologies is key to delivering the Government’s mission on growth.

rows.headline: John Edwards speaks at ICO’s event with the AI APPG in Parliament
rows.title: John Edwards speaks at ICO’s event with the AI APPG in Parliament

rows.title: ICO consultation on the draft updated guidance on encryption
